2005-08-01.title: Spacewalk may be needed for repair
Violence breaks out as Sudanese vice president's death is confirmed
Far beyond Pluto, a possible 10th planet is found
Briefly: Bush fitness 'superior,' annual checkup finds

2005-08-02.title: New draft moves Korea talks forward
With Fahd's demise, a swift succession
U.S. envoy is arriving at changed world body
Governor plans to veto 'morning after' bill
Sudan clashes continue after death of rebel leader

2005-08-03.title: Britain Indicts Suspect Tied to Failed Attack
2005-08-03.abstract: The British police announced that they had indicted a person held in connection with the attempted July 21 bombings.

2005-08-03.title: Zimbabwe Drops Treason Case Against Mugabe Opponent
2005-08-03.abstract: Without explanation, the government dropped its treason case against Morgan Tsvangirai, the leader of the main opposition party, scrapping a two-year-long prosecution on charges that carried a potential death penalty. Mr. Tsvangirai was charged with treason after he organized demonstrations against President Robert G. Mugabe in 2003. Prosecutors claimed the protests were an attempt to overthrow the government. In October, Mr. Tsvangirai was acquitted of a separate charge of plotting to kill Mr. Mugabe. At a news conference yesterday, Mr. Tsvangirai thanked supporters for their efforts ''during the period of relentless persecution'' by the government.   Sharon LaFraniere (NYT)

2005-08-03.title: World Briefing: Africa; Europe; Americas; Middle East
Iran's president-elect receives religious approval to take office

2005-08-04.title: U.S. reporter is abducted and found dead in Iraq
Bush Responds to Al Qaeda Tape, Saying U.S. Fight Will Continue
2005-08-04.abstract: President Bush said that videotape threats from Al Qaeda's No. 2 will not drive the U.S. out of Iraq or the Mideast.

2005-08-04.title: For London's Police Chief, Plaudits, and Barbs, in Bombings' Wake
2005-08-04.abstract: Shortly before bombers struck London on July 7, Sir Ian Blair, the city's police chief, gave a radio interview commending the force he had commanded for just five months as the ''envy of the policing world in relation to counterterrorism.''     Four hours later, Sir Ian was appealing for Londoners to stay calm after an attack on three subway trains and a red double-decker bus that killed 56 people, including four bombers. From the calm of a radio studio, Sir Ian had been plunged into a mayhem of bloodshed and sirens in a stricken city. His boast of a few hours earlier seemed hollow. His mien was ashen.

2005-08-04.title: Riot Toll Mounts in Sudan After Rebel Leader's Death
2005-08-04.abstract: Rioting and tit-for-tat killings continued in Sudan's capital, Khartoum, on Wednesday, as southerners upset with the death of the rebel leader John Garang clashed with northern Arabs and government security forces struggling to restore order.     The death toll from three days of unrest in Khartoum and its suburbs approached 100, according to local authorities and relief agencies. There were reports of gangs of people, some carrying clubs, knives and guns, marauding through the streets, even well after a government-imposed dusk-to-dawn curfew.

2005-08-04.title: New Saudi king receives ritual honors

2005-08-05.title: Europeans prepare broad deal for Iran
Offer by Europe Would Give Iran Nuclear Future
2005-08-05.abstract: European negotiators will offer Iran chance to achieve full political and economic relationship with West, and possibility of getting nuclear reactors and fuel, if it ends nuclear activities suspected to be part of weapons program; European offer, drafted with tacit approval of Bush administtration, will be first test of new leadership in Iran; there is considerable doubt that Iran will accept proposal, at least right away, since it seeks Iran's pledge to end uranium conversion and enrichment activities; diplomats note that proposal contains face-saving provision for Iran conceding right to certain activities it is being asked to give up; although uranium enrichment is allowed under Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty, West's view is that Iran has forfeited that right because it has engaged in clandestine activities; Iran's anger at being asked to give up activities allowed to other countries is at heart of its repeated announcment that it will resume such work when it chooses (M)

2005-08-05.title: Russian military race to save trapped submarine
Blair Vows New Laws to End Sanctuary for Muslim Extremists
2005-08-05.abstract: With promises to close down mosques and deport clerics deemed to be fostering violence, Britain seemed to move closer to measures adopted in the U.S. after 9/11.

2005-08-06.title: Ugandan leader raises doubts on Garang death
Israel lobbyists charged with disclosing secrets
Blair vows to expel preachers of hatred
Some Bombs Used in Iraq Are Made in Iran, U.S. Says
2005-08-06.abstract: Many of the new, more sophisticated roadside bombs used to attack American and government forces in Iraq have been designed in Iran and shipped in from there, United States military and intelligence officials said Friday, raising the prospect of increased foreign help for Iraqi insurgents.     American commanders say the deadlier bombs could become more common as insurgent bomb makers learn the techniques to make the weapons themselves in Iraq.

2005-08-06.title: Blair Is Seeking to Curb Radicals Who Preach Hate
2005-08-06.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair promised new measures on Friday to close down mosques and bar or deport clerics deemed to be fostering hatred and violence, bringing Britain's antiterrorism policy more into line with some of its neighbors' and answering critics who say the country has sheltered Islamic extremists for years.     He also said two Islamic organizations would be banned. A global list would be drawn up of people ''whose activities or views pose a threat to Britain's security,'' and they would be kept out of Britain.

2005-08-07.title: Niger's Nomads Agonize as Livestock Die
2005-08-07.abstract: With his black burnoose and piercing tan eyes set in angular, leathery features, Ali Yougouda is the very picture of a Tuareg, a stoic nomad who juggles two wives, 10 children and life on the Sahara's fringes without breaking a sweat.      Until he talks about his herd. In May, he was tending 68 head of cattle and sheep. Today he has 18 cows and bulls. He is devastated, bereft.

2005-08-07.title: An American in Chile Finds Conservation a Hard Slog
2005-08-07.abstract: Douglas Tompkins first fell under the spell of Chilean Patagonia's lush forests and rushing streams as a backpacking teenager from New York. Today, 40-odd years older and much, much richer, he may well be the region's biggest individual landowner, and its most controversial foreign presence.     His holdings in this realm of snowcapped peaks and wind-whipped shores are larger than Rhode Island and include tracts that timber, electric power and agricultural interests covet. But instead of moving to chop, dam and dig, Mr. Tompkins has turned his properties into nature sanctuaries, open to the public but with strict limits on use, that he has said repeatedly he hopes to donate to the people of Chile. If they can be persuaded to accept his offer.

2005-08-07.title: Former Rebel Leader Buried As Sudanese Pay Tribute
2005-08-07.abstract: Vast crowds of Sudanese paid their last respects to John Garang, the former rebel leader who helped forge peace in Sudan.

2005-08-07.title: 13 dead after planes crashes off Sicily coast
Security Fears Prompt U.S. to Close Saudi Embassy
2005-08-07.abstract: United States diplomatic missions in Saudi Arabia will be closed for at least two days in response to possible threats against American buildings.

2005-08-08.title: Iran Resumes Nuclear Work, a Step That May Lead to Sanctions
2005-08-08.abstract: Iran acted despite European threats to refer the case to the United Nations Security Council for punitive action.

2005-08-08.title: Netanyahu quits over the pullout from Gaza
Bosnian Serb War Fugitive Arrested in Argentina
2005-08-08.abstract: After more than five years on the run, Milan Lukic, a Bosnian Serb militia leader wanted by the United Nations war crimes tribunal, was arrested in Buenos Aires.

2005-08-08.title: Japanese Cabinet moves to dissolve parliament
U.N. Inquiry Says Oil-for-Food Chief Accepted Kickbacks
2005-08-08.abstract: The report today was the first to accuse United Nations officials of outright corruption and personal benefit.

2005-08-09.title: Atomic Activity Resumes in Iran Amid Warnings
2005-08-09.abstract: Iran acted despite European threats to refer the case to the United Nations Security Council for punitive action.

2005-08-09.title: Nagasaki marks 60th anniversary of atomic bombing
Britain Renews Terror Warning in Saudi Arabia
2005-08-09.abstract: Foreign Office in Britain issues renewed warnings that attacks by terrorists might be imminent in Saudi Arabia, echoing warning by United States that prompted it to close its diplomatic missions there; Foreign Office warns Britons who stay in Saudi Arabia to maintain high level of vigilance; Australian department of foreign affairs warns its citizens to avoid travel to Saudi Arabia; King Abdullah bin Abdel Aziz, who assumed throne Aug 1, vows to continue aggressive campaign against militants (M)

2005-08-09.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Study Finds No Spread Of Altered Corn
2005-08-09.abstract: A broad sampling of maize in Oaxaca State, where genetically modified corn was detected five years ago, has failed to find any transgenic seeds, leading researchers from Mexico and the United States to conclude that the bioengineered seeds had not spread widely. Their report, being published online this week in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (www.pnas.org) is the largest study yet of the presence of transgenic corn in Oaxaca. ''Despite the fact that Mexico imports transgenic maize grain for food, feed and processing, and despite the expectation that some of this grain may be planted in farmers' fields, the introgression of transgenes in the sampled area appeared to be negligible in 2003 and 2004,'' the researchers said. Mexico banned planting of genetically modified corn in 1998.  Elisabeth Malkin (NYT)

2005-08-10.title: Why Baghdad Must Make Do With Takeout
2005-08-10.abstract: Chen Xianzhong, proprietor of Baghdad's first authentic Chinese restaurant in new Iraq, explains that since recent suicide bombing outside he is doing takeout only for few loyal customers; Chen, who studied Arabic in China, has worked at various ventures in region, for Chinese government and for himself, eventually opening two restaurants and hotel; photo; business thrived until kidnapping and carjacking attacks on his workers and himself; Chen says he is trying to recoup his money and still likes Iraq, where he earned his first US dollar (M)

2005-08-10.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Criminal Inquiry Into Sub Accident
2005-08-10.abstract: Military prosecutors opened a criminal investigation into the ordeal of the Russian submarine trapped for more than three days under the Pacific Ocean. A statement by the Pacific Fleet's military prosecutor cited ''legal violations in the activities of certain military officials,'' including the decision to dispatch the submarine, known as the AS-28 Priz, without a second submarine as a backup, Interfax reported. A crewless British submersible rescued the submarine and the seven men aboard on Sunday after cutting it free from fishing nets off the Kamchatka Peninsula. The Ministry of Defense, facing criticism for its lack of preparedness, was reported to be considering buying one or more of the British submersibles.   Steven Lee Myers (NYT)

2005-08-10.title: Sharon Criticizes Minister for Quitting Before Gaza Pullout
2005-08-10.abstract: Also, thousands of Jews gathered at the Western Wall tonight to pray for a halt to Israel's pullout, scheduled for next week.

2005-08-10.title: Names of the Dead
2005-08-10.abstract: Names of five Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq; 1,826 American service members have died since start of war

2005-08-10.title: Iran Rejects Halt in Atom Activity, but Is Likely to Continue Talks
2005-08-10.abstract: Iran rejects European calls to halt renewed nuclear activities but indicates willingness to continue negotiations; International Atomic Energy Agency holds emergency board meeting in Vienna; consensus on resolution is likely to be thwarted by developing nations; new Pres Mahmoud Ahmadinejad calls uranium processing 'our right,' but tells Sec Gen Kofi Annan he will continue talks although Iran rejects Europe's current offer; Russia demands Iran halt uranium work, while France and Germany see more negotiations (M)

2005-08-11.title: Briefly: 14 die as helicopter crashes off Estonia
Just When You Thought Schröder Was History
2005-08-11.abstract: Letter From Europe article on Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, who faces elections in five weeks and now is suddenly no longer being decreed a sure loser; candidate expected to defeat him, Angela Merkel, has been faltering; photos (M)

2005-08-11.title: Egypt Certifies 9 Challengers to Mubarak in Coming Election
2005-08-11.abstract: The race for the presidency in September has been both hailed as a first step toward democracy and vilified as hoax and a sham.

2005-08-11.title: Malaysia, Blanketed in Noxious Haze, Declares Emergency
2005-08-11.abstract: Smoke from forest fires in Indonesia blanketed parts of the peninsula, forcing the country's biggest seaport to shut down.

2005-08-11.title: Uruguay Acknowledges Role in Argentina's 'Disappeared'
2005-08-11.abstract: The Uruguay Air Force has acknowledged that it operated flights from Argentina that transported political prisoners who were killed during the 1973-85 military dictatorship.

2005-08-12.title: Iran rejects UN nuclear concerns as 'absurd'
Rebels kill Afghan woman after calling her a U.S. spy
Britain Plans to Deport 10 Foreigners as Security Threat
2005-08-12.abstract: British home secretary Charles Clarke says 10 foreigners seen as 'threat to national security' have beeen seized and would be deported; says foreigners, whom he declines to name, were detained in raids by police in London, Leicester, Luton and Birmingham, all cities with substantial Muslim minorities; this is first such move since July bombings in London; Home Office says those detained would be allowed to appeal, but civil rights groups express fear that foreigners could be sent to countries that use torture or other abuses, in violation of European human rights rules; in Beirut, Lebanese official says British-based militant cleric Omar Bakri Mohammed has been detained; he left Britain day after Prime Min Tony Blair announced broad range of new anti-terrorism measures, including deportation of clerics fomenting violence (M)

2005-08-12.title: Rushdie appeals for a Reformation in Islam

2005-08-13.title: Names of the Dead
2005-08-13.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,840 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans yesterday:    CARRASQUILLO, Miguel, 25, Specialist, Army; River Grove, Ill.; Third Infantry Division.    HERRERA, Evenor C., 22, Lance Cpl., Marine Corps; Gypsum, Colo.; First Marine Division.    KALLADEEN, Anthony N., 26, Specialist, Army National Guard; Purchase, N.Y.; 69th Infantry Regiment, 256th Brigade Combat Team.    OSTROM Ryan S., 25, Staff Sgt., Army National Guard; Liberty, Pa.; First Battalion, 109th Infantry Regiment.    RIOS, Hernando, 29, Pfc., Army National Guard; Queens; 69th Infantry Regiment, 256th Brigade Combat Team.

2005-08-13.title: This Johnny Appleseed Is Wanted by the Law
2005-08-13.abstract: FRESHLY released on bail, Marc Emery faced the camera of his Pot-TV.net Web site the other day to make an urgent appeal for money to finance his legal struggle to avert extradition to the United States for trafficking marijuana seeds south of the border.     ''Let me be the light that shines on the American gulag,'' he said, stern-eyed, pointing into the camera. Without notes, Mr. Emery sermonized for a half-hour about everything from the marvelous medicinal and spiritual qualities of pot to the greatness of Thomas Jefferson, ''who gave America on hemp paper the Declaration of Independence.''

2005-08-13.title: Muslims tout assimilation in north Thailand
Malaysia to send help for Indonesia's fires
G.I.'s Deployed in Iraq Desert With Lots of American Stuff
2005-08-13.abstract: First Lt. Taysha Deaton of the Louisiana National Guard went to war expecting a gritty yearlong deployment of sand, heat and duress, but ended up spending her nights in a king-size bed beneath imported sheets and a fluffy down comforter.     She bought the bed from a departing soldier to replace the twin-size metal frame that came with her air-conditioned trailer on this base in western Baghdad. She also acquired a refrigerator, television, cellphone, microwave oven, boom box and DVD player, and signed up for a high-speed Internet connection.

2005-08-14.title: U.S. Builds Pressure for Iraq Constitution as Deadline Nears
2005-08-14.abstract: The United States ratcheted up its pressure Saturday on Iraqi negotiators who are trying to meet a deadline for writing a draft constitution set for Monday.      Speaking by cellphone from a meeting with Zalmay Khalilzad, the American ambassador to Iraq, a Sunni Arab member of the Iraqi constitutional committee compared the document to a meal that had not finished cooking. ''We are still in the kitchen now, but there is nothing yet,'' said the Sunni member, Haseeb Aref. ''There are only pressures on us.'' Mr. Aref then apologized and said because of the meeting with the ambassador he did not have time to say anything else. But another Sunni member of the committee, Saleh A. Mutlak, said that most of the American pressure was expended on urging the Sunnis to agree to the demands of the other two major parties, the Shiite Arabs and the Kurds.

2005-08-14.title: British Airways resumes flights, but many remain stranded
Bakiyev sworn in as new president of Kyrgyzstan
Iranian Cabinet nominees include several hard-liners

2005-08-15.title: Israeli Forces Encircle Outposts Defying Order to Leave Gaza
2005-08-15.abstract: Thousands of Israeli forces told residents that they have until Wednesday to leave before they are pulled out of their homes.

2005-08-15.title: Thousands hold out in Gaza against evacuation
Iraqi politicians scramble to reach agreement on constitution
Indonesia and Aceh prepare for peace

2005-08-16.title: Clues Sought to Cause of Puzzling Air Crash in Greece
2005-08-16.abstract: Investigators into Helios Airways jet's mysterious crash in Greece turn to physical and electronic evidence for clues, because there was no radio contact from plane for 90 minutes before accident; Greek officials say whatever happened did not force down Boeing 737 immediately, but killed or disabled at least one of pilots, then sent plane on slow decline that ended in crash into mountains north of Athens; plane's badly damaged second black box has been found; autopsies begin on victims; two main theories about crash: that plane suffered loss of pressure or that toxic fumes overwhelmed those aboard; photo (M)

2005-08-16.title: Sinai Blast Hurts 2 Peacekeepers
2005-08-16.abstract: Two Canadian peacekeepers suffer minor injuries when roadside bomb explodes less than mile from Sinai military base where international troops are stationed to monitor peace accord between Egypt and Israel (S)

2005-08-16.title: Despite gunshots, mother vows to continue antiwar vigil
Israel acts to press settlers to leave Gaza

2005-08-17.title: World Briefing | Africa: Nigeria: 102 Dead In Boat Accident
2005-08-17.abstract: A wooden ferry capsized in the rain-swollen Jalingo River in northern Nigeria, and although the police initially stated that 21 passengers had been rescued, later reports indicated that all 102 people on the boat had been swept away and drowned. A regional police official told The Associated Press that passengers had panicked when the boat began to take on water and that their rush for a safe place caused the vessel to overturn. Last week, 60 Nigerians died when floodwaters collapsed a bridge on the same river in Taraba State, not far from the scene of the ferry disaster.  Michael Wines (NYT)

2005-08-17.title: Israeli Troops Persuade, and Force, Settlers to Leave Gaza
2005-08-17.abstract: Some had to be dragged out of their homes and loaded onto buses. Also, an Israeli killed two Palestinians in the West Bank.

2005-08-17.title: 17 Killed in Crash of Spanish Military Copter in Afghanistan
2005-08-17.abstract: A Spanish military helicopter crashed in the desert in western Afghanistan on Tuesday, killing all 17 on board, in what military officials said appeared to be an accident. A second helicopter made a forced landing in the same area, injuring 5 of the 17 Spanish soldiers on board, the NATO-led International Security and Assistance Force said in a statement.     ''ISAF believes that this is the result of an accident, but will not prejudge the results of the investigation,'' said Maj. Andrew Elmes, a spokesman for the force in Kabul.

2005-08-17.title: Destruction of crosses angers fallen GI's mother
Roberts sided with Rehnquist on school prayer, papers show

2005-08-18.title: Pope Arrives in Germany for 4-Day Youth Festival
2005-08-18.abstract: Benedict XVI returned today to his "beloved homeland" of Germany, in his first trip abroad as pope.

2005-08-18.title: Pope to Sound Theme of Non-Christian Ties at Festival
2005-08-18.abstract: Pope Benedict XVI is expected to make renewal of Europe's sense of its Christian roots his central theme on his first trip abroad as pope, to festival of young Roman Catholics in Cologne in his native Germany; he is expected to meet there with leaders of other faiths; event is being seen as test of new papcy's stance twoard other faiths at time of atrain and suspicion on all sides; photos (M)

2005-08-18.title: Roh urges early end to atomic crisis

2005-08-19.title: Mo Mowlam, former UK Northern Ireland chief, dies
Letter from Asia: 'China first' approach: A missed opportunity
At Synagogue, Pope Warns of Rising Anti-Semitism
2005-08-19.abstract: Pope Benedict XVI won a standing ovation during a visit to a rebuilt synagogue that had been destroyed by the Nazis.

2005-08-19.title: Liberals lean to opposing confirmation of Roberts
British Police Accused of Resisting Inquiry
2005-08-19.abstract: The independent agency investigating the death of a Brazilian electrician who was mistaken for a terrorist and killed by the police last month said the police department tried to resist its investigation, causing delays. Sir Ian Blair, commissioner of the Metropolitan Police, rejected the claim, saying that there had been ''no cover-up.'' The electrician, 27-year-old Jean Charles de Menezes, left, was shot seven times in the head on July 22 after boarding a subway train. The police claimed that he had worn an unseasonably bulky coat and had acted suspiciously, but documents from the Independent Police Complaints Commission investigation that were leaked to ITV television contradict much of the official explanation.  Sarah Lyall (NYT)

2005-08-20.title: Letter from Russia: Even during dacha days, ripples of popular anger
In a Lawless Town, the Top Lawman Avoids Trouble
2005-08-20.abstract: OMAR PIMENTEL'S office seemed more like a secluded getaway than a police command post at the center of a drug war. Its walls were painted the blue-green color of the Caribbean Sea, and a mix of meditation music and New Age rock played on the stereo. There were no police scanners, no crime reports, no medals of honor, not a piece of clutter anywhere, except for a jar of Hershey's Kisses and a dopey-looking doll of a character from the children's movie ''Monsters, Inc.,'' with its twiggy limbs and a frightened single eye.     There was fear in Mr. Pimentel's eyes, too, on a recent day. He is the new police chief in the new murder capital of the United States-Mexico border. The authorities report almost a killing a day in this city of 330,000 people, as rival drug gangs fight for control of the lucrative routes that run through here into Texas. Many of the dead have been police officers, most notably Mr. Pimentel's immediate predecessor, Alejandro Domínguez, who was killed in a hail of gunfire his first day on duty.

2005-08-20.title: Pope Visits German Synagogue and Warns of Growing Anti-Semitism
2005-08-20.abstract: Pope Benedict XVI, who was drafted into the German Army during World War II, visited a synagogue here on Friday that had been destroyed by the Nazis and warned of a growing anti-Semitism that he called a ''reason for concern and vigilance.''     ''Today, sadly, we are witnessing the rise of new signs of anti-Semitism and various forms of a general hostility toward foreigners,'' the pope said in a reverent hourlong visit to the Cologne synagogue on his first trip abroad. ''The Catholic Church is committed -- and I affirm this again today -- to tolerance, respect, friendship and peace between all peoples, cultures and religions.''

2005-08-20.title: 6 autopsies provide clues in Athens crash
World Briefings: Americas, Asia, Africa, Europe

2005-08-21.title: Police Defend Handling of London Subway Shooting
2005-08-21.abstract: The head of the London police said today that he had not known until 24 hours after the killing of a Brazilian man by police officers that the man had been an innocent bystander.

2005-08-21.title: Iraqi Talks Move Ahead on Some Issues
2005-08-21.abstract: Iraqi leaders trying to complete a new constitution moved Saturday toward deals on such contentious issues as Shiite autonomy, sharing oil revenues and Kurdish self-rule. But as they progressed on those fronts, a tentative agreement that would have given Islam an expanded role in the state and in family disputes appeared to unravel.     ''Islam is back on the table,'' said a person close to the negotiations, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, citing the sensitivity of the talks.

2005-08-21.title: Bush Confidante Begins Task of Repairing America's Image Abroad
2005-08-21.abstract: For years, President Bush has called on Karen P. Hughes, his confidante from Texas, to help devise replies to attacks from political foes. Now Ms. Hughes, installed at the State Department, plans to set up ''rapid response'' teams to counter bad news and defend administration policies around the globe.     The teams, to be set up in the Middle East and elsewhere, are one of several initiatives being prepared by Ms. Hughes, who took office this week as under secretary of public diplomacy. The initiatives are part of what Bush administration officials say will be an aggressive drive to repair America's poor image abroad, particularly in Muslim countries.

2005-08-21.title: Roadside Bomb Kills 4 U.S. Soldiers in Afghanistan
2005-08-21.abstract: Americans came under attack in two separate incidents today that highlighted the rising violence in the country just one month ahead of parliamentary elections.

2005-08-21.title: As Sunni Divisions Widen, Iraq's Sufis Are Under Attack
2005-08-21.abstract: As the twilight ritual of the Sufi Muslims reached its crescendo, the five drummers pounded harder and quicker, inspiring the men standing in a circle to spin their heads ever more rapidly, their shoulder-length hair twirling through the air.     The sun dipped low beyond the shrine's inner courtyard, and the chanting rose in volume.

2005-08-22.title: White House Letter: In parallel universes, but still worlds apart
Iraq Accuses Jordan of Allowing Financing of Insurgency
2005-08-22.abstract: The Iraqi government accused Jordan on Sunday of allowing family members of Saddam Hussein to finance an insurgent campaign to destabilize Iraq and restore the Baath Party.      ''I am sorry to say there are a lot of figures, not only senior regime members, but those who supervise the terrorist groups, in Jordan,'' Laith Kubba, a spokesman for Prime Minister Ibrahim al-Jaafari, told reporters.

2005-08-22.title: G.I. Death Toll in Afghanistan Worst Since '01
2005-08-22.abstract: United States is having its deadliest year in Afghanistan since 2001--65 soldiers killed so far this year--and violence is expected to intensify before nation's legislative elections Sept 18; 13 soldiers are killed in Aug alone; latest four are killed Aug 21 when roadside bomb hits their vehicle in south; two United States Embassy employees are wounded when their convoy is hit by explosion close to Kabul; Afghan general says some fighters want to disrupt elections, while others are coming across border from Pakistan to help ousted Taliban or Al Qaeda with long-term aim of dislodging American troops from Afghanistan; notes Taliban claim to have new antiaircraft missiles; senior security official says more money is coming in, probably from Arab countries, and unit of Qaeda fighters has returned to region; in addition to attacks on election workers and candidates, more than six clerics and tribal elders have been killed since May; photo (M)

2005-08-22.title: Bush names envoyfor rights in N. Korea

2005-08-23.title: Out of Gaza and into the West Bank
World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Small Quake Shakes Rome
Last settlers leave Gaza but vow they'll return
Opposition Party's Angry Slogan Gets Cairo's Official O.K.
2005-08-23.abstract: It is the Arabic language equivalent of an angry spouse slamming the door and shouting, ''I've had it with you!''     The decision of an opposition political party, the Wafd Party, to use one word -- which literally means, ''We have been suffocated'' -- as a political slogan, and the government's permission for its use, has created a huge stir in Egypt's first multicandidate presidential elections.

2005-08-24.title: Bush goes on attack to defend Iraq policy
World Briefing: Africa; Middle East; Europe; Asia
State Dept. Pushes for Return Of North Korea to Negotiations
2005-08-24.abstract: Bush administration steps up diplomacy to get North Korea back to talks on dismantling its nuclear programs, with no sign of progress; Sec Condoleezza Rice meets with South Korean Min Ban Ki Moon in Washington; negotiator Christopher Hill says there is Chinese draft condensing various principles put forth by other parties to talks (M)

2005-08-24.title: Land of 74,000 Protests (but Little Is Ever Fixed)
2005-08-24.abstract: Letter From Asia on growing air of uneasiness in China, after months of increasingly bold protests rolling across countryside, over issues from rampant industrial pollution to evictions and land seizures by corrupt local governments; photos (M)

2005-08-24.title: Bush Says Leaving Iraq Now Would Be Wrong
2005-08-24.abstract: Pres Bush says war protesters like Cindy Sheehan who want immediate US withdrawal from Iraq are advocating policy that would weaken US and also do not represent views of other military families he has met; comments at resort in Idaho; low-key protest in Boise hears Navy widow Melanie House call for troops to come home; antiwar group Gold Star Families for Peace begins running television ad featuring Sheehan; photo (M)

2005-08-25.title: Briefly: Florida coast warned of possible hurricane
Israel is set to seize land for wall in West Bank
Abbas Says Raid in West Bank Undermines Peace Efforts
2005-08-25.abstract: Five Palestinians were killed, including an Islamic Jihad leader who had orchestrated two bombings, Israel's military said.

2005-08-25.title: EU officials in China to fix flawed textile pact

2005-08-26.title: Blast Injures Regional Leader in Russia
2005-08-26.abstract: Two explosions in quick succession struck the motorcade of a senior official in Ingushetia, a restive region bordering Chechnya, killing a bodyguard and wounding three others.

2005-08-26.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Exiled Muslim Accused Of Terror Plot
World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Blast Injures Regional Leader
Death toll in European flooding mounts to 42

2005-08-27.title: World Briefing | Africa: Tuberculosis Emergency Declared
World Briefing | Europe: Germany: 3 Chechens Held After Manhunt
World Briefings: Europe, Africa, Asia, Americas
Burmese Power Struggle May Be Heating Up, or Not
2005-08-27.abstract: Wishful thinking, said U Aung Zaw, an émigré journalist from Myanmar, who has seen it all before, many times.     Rumors of a coup among the generals who rule that closed and repressive country flared briefly this week, then died away just as suddenly.

2005-08-27.title: Israel will maintain control over Gaza's borders, Sharon aide says

2005-08-28.title: Israel Suffers First Suicide Attack Since Gaza Pullout
2005-08-28.abstract: Hours later, however, the Israeli cabinet voted to allow Egypt to patrol the international border with Gaza, which would allow Israel to pull all its soldiers out of Gaza in the next month.

2005-08-28.title: Language Born of Colonialism Thrives Again in Amazon
2005-08-28.abstract: When the Portuguese arrived in Brazil five centuries ago, they encountered a fundamental problem: the indigenous peoples they conquered spoke more than 700 languages. Rising to the challenge, the Jesuit priests accompanying them concocted a mixture of Indian, Portuguese and African words they called ''língua geral,'' or the ''general language,'' and imposed it on their colonial subjects.     Elsewhere in Brazil, língua geral as a living, spoken tongue died off long ago. But in this remote and neglected corner of the Amazon where Brazil, Colombia and Venezuela meet, the language has not only managed to survive, it has made a remarkable comeback in recent years.

2005-08-28.title: Neglected Poor in Africa Make Their Own Safety Nets
2005-08-28.abstract: Nogaye Sow is a humble street vendor in a rough patch of urban Africa, but hidden in her flowing robe is a weathered piece of cardboard that helps put her on equal footing with those who work in air-conditioned offices instead of at the curb.      It is a makeshift health insurance card, with photographs of her, her seven children, her granddaughter and two other relatives pasted inside. When they get sick, they receive free consultations at the clinic down the road, cut-rate medicine and peace of mind. The chances are lower now that a bout of illness will bring the family to total ruin.

2005-08-28.title: Constitution Sent to Parliament in Iraq Despite Sunni Objections
2005-08-28.abstract: Iraqi leaders presented a disputed constitution to Parliament, setting the stage for a protracted period of political conflict.

World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Mugabe Risks I.M.F. Expulsion
2005-08-31.abstract: President Robert G. Mugabe ''simply does not care'' whether the International Monetary Fund chooses to expel his nation because it is $290 million in arrears on payment of the more than $1 billion it owes the agency, the Johannesburg newspaper The Star quoted one of his top aides as saying. The aide said Mr. Mugabe refused to meet an I.M.F. team that visited Harare last week to gather information in advance of a meeting at which Zimbabwe could face expulsion for refusal to pay its debts. South Africa has been negotiating a bailout of Zimbabwe's debt with Mr. Mugabe, but his rejection of the fund has complicated those talks, the newspaper reported. He is said to have refused conditions, including movement toward political and economic reforms, that President Thabo Mbeki of South Africa hoped to place on the money.    Michael Wines (NYT)

2005-08-31.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ukraine: $2 Million From U.S. To Destroy Weapons
2005-08-31.abstract: Two American senators, Richard G. Lugar, Republican of Indiana, and Barack Obama, Democrat of Illinois, urged Ukraine to pass legislation enabling the destruction of its vast stockpile of conventional weapons, which could end up in terrorists' hands. Ukraine has at least two million tons of conventional ordnance and seven million excess small-caliber weapons, according to NATO, which has sponsored a program to accelerate their destruction. The United States has pledged $2.1 million for the program's early phase.   C.J. Chivers (NYT)
